Ching Shih: The prostitute who commanded 80,000 pirates and made the Chinese Empire surrender to her terms
Rasputin's assassination: the 'unkillable monk' who survived cyanide, bullets, and drowning before actually dying from hypothermia
Emperor Elagabalus: Rome's 14-year-old transgender priest-emperor who married a Vestal Virgin, prostituted himself in the palace, and got murdered in a latrine after 4 years of chaos (218-222 AD)
Sultan Ibrahim I of the Ottoman Empire: The 'Mad Sultan' who drowned 280 concubines in weighted sacks because one might have slept with another man (1640s)

Emperor Qin Shi Huang's mercury tomb: The megalomaniac who unified China, sought immortality by drinking mercury daily, and built a mausoleum so toxic with liquid mercury rivers that excavation would kill the archaeologists (210 BC)

Timur (Tamerlane) the Great's skull pyramid at Baghdad 1401: The conqueror who built a 90,000-skull tower, played chess during a siege while his engineers invented firearms, and whose exhumed corpse in 1941 may have triggered Hitler's invasion of the USSR

Emperor Valerian (253-260 AD): Rome's only emperor captured alive in battle, who spent his final years as Persian King Shapur I's human footstool and was later skinned, stuffed with straw, and displayed in a temple for centuries

Mehmed II's 'Blood Tax' (Devshirme System, 1453-1512): The Ottoman sultan who kidnapped Christian boys, converted them into the empire's elite killers (Janissaries), then watched them strangle his own son to death by silk cord in 1512

Modu Chanyu (Mete Han) 209 BC: The Xiongnu warlord who invented whistling arrows to train his army to kill anyone he pointed at—then used them to execute his own father, favorite wife, and best horse to prove absolute obedience before conquering Central Asia

Sultan Murad IV (1623-1640): The Ottoman sultan who executed 25,000 people in his 17-year reign, personally strangled musicians for playing during Ramadan, disguised himself to patrol taverns and behead drinkers on the spot, then died at 27 from alcohol-induced cirrhosis

Empress Irene of Byzantium (797-802 AD): The mother who blinded her own son with red-hot irons to become Rome's first sole female emperor, then got overthrown and exiled for refusing to marry Charlemagne

Emperor Majorian (457-461 AD): The last great Roman emperor who rebuilt the Western Empire's navy, defeated barbarian kings, and was about to reconquer North Africa—then got ambushed and beheaded by his own general Ricimer, who sold the empire piece by piece to Germanic warlords

Emperor Elagabalus's grandmother Julia Maesa (218-224 AD): The Syrian powerbroker who overthrew Emperor Macrinus by bribing Rome's legions with her dead sister's fortune, installed her 14-year-old grandson as emperor, then watched him prostitute himself in the palace before orchestrating his assassination and replacing him with her other grandson

Emperor Hongwu (Zhu Yuanzhang) 1368-1398: The beggar-turned-emperor who purged 100,000+ officials in paranoid terror campaigns, skinned corrupt bureaucrats and displayed their hides in government offices as warnings, and ordered his concubines buried alive with his corpse

Emperor Basil II 'the Bulgar-Slayer' (1014 AD): The Byzantine emperor who captured 15,000 Bulgarian soldiers at the Battle of Kleidion, blinded 99 out of every 100 men, left one eye on every hundredth soldier to lead the others home, and caused Tsar Samuel to die of shock when he saw his mutilated army

Emperor Phocas (602-610 AD): The centurion who murdered Emperor Maurice and his five sons, turned Byzantium into a torture state, watched the Persians conquer Syria and Egypt while he mutilated aristocrats in the Hippodrome, then got his genitals crushed, limbs hacked off, and head paraded through Constantinople on a pike

Empress Theodora of Byzantium (500-548 AD): The prostitute-turned-empress who rose from a bear trainer's daughter performing sexual acts on stage to co-ruler of Rome, passed laws protecting women from forced prostitution, and allegedly orchestrated the massacre of 30,000 rioters in the Hippodrome when her husband wanted to flee

Emperor Justinian's Nika Riots (532 AD): The chariot-racing mob that burned half of Constantinople, nearly toppled the throne, and got crushed when Justinian's general Belisarius trapped 30,000 screaming fans inside the Hippodrome and slaughtered them all in three hours

Battle of Carrhae 53 BC: Marcus Licinius Crassus—Rome's richest man and third of the Triumvirate—got 20,000 legionaries slaughtered by Parthian horse archers, captured alive, and executed by having molten gold poured down his throat to mock his greed

Battle of Manzikert 1071 AD: Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes—betrayed by his own generals mid-battle, captured alive by Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan, treated with honor for a week, then released with a ransom treaty—only to be overthrown, blinded with red-hot irons by his stepson, and left to die of infected eye sockets in a monastery

Battle of Ankara 1402: Bayezid I 'The Thunderbolt'—the Ottoman sultan who defeated four Crusades and conquered the Balkans—gets crushed by Timur's war elephants, captured alive, paraded in an iron cage across Central Asia, and dies in captivity after smashing his skull against the bars while his son watches

Emperor Aurelian's Assassination 275 AD: The general who reunited Rome's three fractured empires in five years, defeated Zenobia and the Palmyrene Empire, built walls around Rome for the first time in 600 years admitting the capital was vulnerable, then got stabbed to death by his own bodyguards over a forged document his secretary fabricated

Battle of Didgori 1121 AD: King David IV of Georgia's 55,000-man army defeats 400,000 Seljuk Turks using 500 Alan mercenary shock cavalry—history's greatest underdog victory ratio that saved Christian Georgia and ended Seljuk dominance in the Caucasus

Battle of Baideng 200 BC: Emperor Gaozu of Han's 320,000-man army surrounded by Modu Chanyu's 40,000 Xiongnu horsemen for 7 days in -40°C winter—escapes only by bribing the Khan's wife with treasure and a promise to send Chinese princesses as sex slaves

Battle of Pliska 811 AD: Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I's 80,000-man army ambushed in a Bulgarian mountain pass—khan Krum slaughtered the entire force, beheaded the emperor on the battlefield, and turned his skull into a jewel-encrusted drinking cup that he forced captured Byzantine nobles to toast from at victory feasts

Noor Inayat Khan (1914-1944): The Sufi princess and children's author who became Britain's first female radio operator in Nazi-occupied France, got betrayed on her first day, survived 10 months of Gestapo torture without breaking, and was executed at Dachau with a single bullet while whispering 'Liberté'
